F5 BigIP.history: F5 Networks was founded in 1996 in Seattle, Washington and went public in 1999. BIG-IP, first introduced in 1997, became the dominant enterprise application delivery controller (ADC) handling load balancing, SSL offload, WAF and traffic management. F5 acquired NGINX in 2019 and rebranded as F5 Inc. in 2021. It is publicly traded on NASDAQ.
F5 BigIP.summary_abuse: F5 BIG-IP is critical network infrastructure used by large enterprises and government organizations. It has no abuse surface itself, but several high-profile CVEs (including CVE-2020-5902 and CVE-2021-22986) have been actively exploited by threat actors to gain access to enterprise networks. F5 responds promptly to vulnerabilities and provides patches. The product is not used as an abuse vehicle itself.

F5 Distributed Cloud Services.history: F5 Distributed Cloud Services was launched in 2021 building on F5's acquisition of Volterra in 2021, combining WAAP, DDoS protection, bot defense, and multi-cloud networking. It integrates F5 BIG-IP and NGINX technologies into a unified SaaS platform. F5 Inc. (NASDAQ: FFIV) is a publicly traded company headquartered in Seattle.
F5 Distributed Cloud Services.summary_abuse: F5 Distributed Cloud Services is an enterprise-grade security and networking platform. It is a defensive infrastructure tool not associated with abuse. As a security-focused platform, it is a target for vulnerability research but F5 has a strong security response program.

Fabric.history: Fabric.js was created by Juriy Zaytsev (kangax) and first released in 2010 as an open-source JavaScript canvas library. It provides an interactive object model on top of the HTML5 canvas element with SVG-to-canvas parsing. The project reached version 6 and then version 7 by 2025 with TypeScript rewrites. It has approximately 31,000 GitHub stars and a large community with over 3,600 forks.
Fabric.summary_abuse: Fabric.js has no known abuse history. It is a client-side canvas rendering library distributed under the MIT license. It does not collect user data, send telemetry, or include any tracking code. The library is fully auditable as open source software and has no dependency on external services.

Facebook Ads.history: Facebook launched its advertising platform in 2007, initially with simple banner ads. Over time it evolved into a sophisticated self-serve system with demographic and behavioral targeting. Following the Cambridge Analytica scandal in 2018, Meta faced significant regulatory scrutiny and implemented changes to targeting options. The platform rebranded under Meta in 2021 and generated $196 billion in ad revenue in 2025.
Facebook Ads.summary_abuse: Facebook Ads is widely abused for counterfeit goods, investment scams, fake crypto opportunities, and celebrity-endorsed fraud schemes. Meta has faced repeated regulatory actions and lawsuits over inadequate fraud prevention. While Meta invests in ad review and automated detection, the scale of the platform and self-serve onboarding means scam ads frequently appear before removal. Meta was fined by the FTC and multiple European regulators over privacy and ad targeting practices.

Facebook Chat Plugin.history: Facebook Chat Plugin, now known as Messenger Customer Chat, is a widget developed by Meta (Facebook) that allows website owners to embed Facebook Messenger into their websites. Launched around 2017, it allows visitors logged into Facebook to chat with businesses directly on their website. The plugin requires visitors to be logged into their Facebook account to use it, linking website behavior to Facebook profiles.
Facebook Chat Plugin.summary_abuse: Facebook Chat Plugin is a significant privacy concern because it links website visitors to their Facebook profiles and reports browsing behavior to Meta. Visitors must be logged into Facebook to chat, enabling cross-site tracking. Meta has faced repeated GDPR investigations and fines across the EU related to its tracking practices. The plugin facilitates collection of website visitor data that Meta uses for advertising targeting.

Facebook Login.history: Facebook Login (OAuth 2.0 social login) was introduced around 2008 and became one of the most widely deployed third-party authentication systems on the web. The Cambridge Analytica scandal in 2018 revealed how third-party apps could harvest user data through the login flow, prompting Meta to restrict data access permissions significantly in 2018-2019.
Facebook Login.summary_abuse: Facebook Login is used for account takeover phishing campaigns where attackers create fake Facebook login pages to steal credentials. When embedded on third-party sites, it sends tracking signals back to Meta regardless of whether users click. The Cambridge Analytica scandal showed systemic data misuse by apps using Facebook Login. Meta has since tightened permissions but the login button still ships Meta's tracking SDK to any page that embeds it.

Facebook Pay.history: Facebook Pay was launched in November 2019 to enable payments across Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and WhatsApp. It was rebranded as Meta Pay in September 2022 following Facebook's rebranding to Meta. It supports peer-to-peer payments within the US and purchases from merchants in the Facebook and Instagram ecosystem.
Facebook Pay.summary_abuse: Meta Pay (formerly Facebook Pay) is used in social commerce contexts within Meta's apps. The platform faces abuse through fake seller accounts on Facebook Marketplace accepting Meta Pay and then not delivering goods. Meta has anti-fraud monitoring but Facebook Marketplace scams are a well-documented problem. The service's integration with Meta's social graph creates unique social engineering vectors.

Facebook Pixel.history: Facebook Pixel was launched by Facebook (now Meta) in 2015 as a JavaScript tracking snippet that webmasters embed in their sites to measure ad conversions and build retargeting audiences. In February 2022, it was rebranded to Meta Pixel to reflect integration with Instagram advertising. It is installed on millions of websites worldwide and is one of the most widely deployed third-party tracking scripts on the internet.
Facebook Pixel.summary_abuse: Facebook Pixel is one of the most privacy-invasive tracking tools on the web. It enables Meta to build cross-site behavioral profiles of internet users without explicit consent. Multiple EU data protection authorities have issued fines and ordered removal of the pixel from healthcare and other sensitive websites. US hospitals have faced lawsuits for sending medical data via the pixel to Meta. The pixel captures page URLs, clicks, form data, and can intercept sensitive health information.

Facebook Pixel Advanced Matching.history: Facebook Pixel Advanced Matching was introduced by Meta in July 2018 to improve ad conversion tracking by hashing and sending PII (email, phone, name) to match website visitors to Facebook profiles. It was created to compensate for increasing browser cookie restrictions and iOS privacy changes. The feature has been central to multiple GDPR and ePrivacy enforcement actions in the EU.
Facebook Pixel Advanced Matching.summary_abuse: Facebook Pixel Advanced Matching hashes and transmits PII from website forms directly to Meta without explicit user consent in many implementations. EU regulators including the Austrian DPA and others have ruled its use illegal under GDPR when operating without proper consent mechanisms. Healthcare providers using the Pixel have been found to inadvertently transmit sensitive medical form data to Meta, prompting FTC and state AG investigations in the US.

Facil-iti.history: FACIL'iti is a French accessibility SaaS company offering over 1,200 digital adaptations for users with cognitive, visual, and motor challenges. Founded in France around 2012, it enables subscribers to create accessibility profiles that adapt any enrolled website's display to their needs. Used by Clarins and other brands.
Facil-iti.summary_abuse: FACIL'iti is a web accessibility overlay tool targeting users with disabilities. Accessibility overlays as a category have been criticized by accessibility experts for not meeting WCAG standards, but FACIL'iti is not associated with phishing or fraud. No notable abuse incidents documented.

Facilita.history: Facilita is a Brazilian CRM platform designed for the real estate market, providing tools to manage client interactions, property listings, and transactions. It serves the Brazilian real estate sector and is available at appfacilita.com.
Facilita.summary_abuse: Facilita is a niche Brazilian real estate CRM. As a B2B SaaS targeting real estate agents and developers in Brazil, it has minimal consumer-facing abuse surface. No notable fraud or phishing incidents documented.

Fact Finder.history: FACT-Finder is a German enterprise ecommerce site search and merchandising platform founded around 2003. It has been the leading European search solution for ecommerce with over 2,000 B2B and B2C shops using it across Europe. The platform uses proprietary AI algorithms refined over 20 years.
Fact Finder.summary_abuse: FACT-Finder is a B2B ecommerce search platform used by established European retailers. As an enterprise tool integrated into existing shop systems, it has no consumer-facing abuse surface. No notable phishing or fraud incidents documented.

Faire.history: Faire was founded in 2017 by Max Rhodes, Marcelo Cortes, Daniele Perito, and Jeff Kolovson, all formerly of Square. The platform creates an online wholesale marketplace connecting independent retailers with brands. It raised significant VC funding growing to a $7B valuation by 2021 and reached $5.2B valuation in December 2025.
Faire.summary_abuse: Faire is a B2B wholesale marketplace connecting independent retailers and brands. As a business marketplace requiring seller and buyer verification, it has lower fraud risk than consumer platforms. Some counterfeit goods risks exist on any marketplace, but Faire's focus on independent brands and retailer vetting mitigates this.

FalguniThemes Nisarg.history: Nisarg is a free GPL WordPress theme created by Falguni Desai (FalguniThemes), an independent developer likely based in India. The theme is designed for travel, photography, and creative blogs. A premium Nisarg PRO version is also available. No major ownership changes documented.
FalguniThemes Nisarg.summary_abuse: Nisarg is a free GPL WordPress theme distributed via WordPress.org. As a standard theme, its main security risk is sites running outdated versions being exploited. No notable abuse or malware distribution documented for this theme.

FameThemes OnePress.history: FameThemes is a small WordPress theme shop offering free and premium themes including OnePress and Screenr. OnePress is a one-page multipurpose business theme with 60K+ active installations. Screenr is a full-screen parallax theme. Both have premium upgrade versions. The company appears to be based in Vietnam.
FameThemes OnePress.summary_abuse: FameThemes produces free GPL-licensed WordPress themes. As standard free themes distributed via WordPress.org, security risks are limited to sites running outdated versions. No notable malware or abuse incidents documented.

FameThemes Screenr.history: FameThemes is a small WordPress theme shop offering free and premium themes including OnePress and Screenr. OnePress is a one-page multipurpose business theme with 60K+ active installations. Screenr is a full-screen parallax theme. Both have premium upgrade versions. The company appears to be based in Vietnam.
FameThemes Screenr.summary_abuse: FameThemes produces free GPL-licensed WordPress themes. As standard free themes distributed via WordPress.org, security risks are limited to sites running outdated versions. No notable malware or abuse incidents documented.

Famewall.history: Famewall is a bootstrapped SaaS tool for collecting and displaying customer testimonials, social proof, and wall-of-love pages. Founded around 2021 as an indie project, it supports importing testimonials from Twitter/X, LinkedIn, Trustpilot, and other platforms. It was featured on Product Hunt.
Famewall.summary_abuse: Famewall is a testimonial collection SaaS. Minimal abuse surface as it embeds customer review widgets. No notable phishing or fraud activity documented.

Famly.history: Famly was founded in 2013 in Copenhagen, Denmark by Anders Laustsen and Henrik Møller Rasmusson. It provides nursery and childcare management software for early childhood education settings. In December 2021 it raised €15.9M led by Susquehanna Growth Equity. It acquired UK-based FirstSteps to expand into the British market.
Famly.summary_abuse: Famly is a childcare management platform handling sensitive data about children and families in nursery settings. As a B2B SaaS with regulated childcare operator customers, it has minimal consumer-facing abuse surface. The platform handles child developmental data which makes data security critical, but no notable breaches or fraud incidents have been documented.

Famous.ai.history: Famous.ai was founded in 2025 by Dr. Alex Mehr in San Francisco as an AI-powered no-code app builder that converts written descriptions into functional mobile and web applications. The platform launched on April 27, 2025, and reported over 130,000 projects created within its first 90 days. Dr. Mehr previously co-founded Zoosk, the online dating company.
Famous.ai.summary_abuse: Famous.ai is a 2025 AI no-code app builder. As with all platforms that let users quickly generate and deploy web and mobile applications, there is theoretical risk of the platform being used to create fraudulent apps or phishing pages at scale. The service is very new and no specific documented abuse incidents are known. The generous free tier and rapid app generation capabilities warrant monitoring as the platform matures.

FancyBox.history: FancyBox is a jQuery/JavaScript lightbox plugin originally created around 2008 for displaying images, videos and multimedia in modal overlays. It evolved through versions 2 and 3 (by fancyapps). The modern version is a TypeScript-based implementation compatible with React, Vue, and plain JavaScript. It uses dual licensing: GPL for open source, commercial for commercial use.
FancyBox.summary_abuse: FancyBox is a widely used open-source lightbox library embedded on millions of websites. As a front-end display component, it has no server-side surface and is not associated with abuse or malicious activity.

Fanplayr.history: Fanplayr was founded around 2012 and is headquartered in Palo Alto, California with offices in New York, Buenos Aires, Milan, London, Amsterdam, Melbourne, and Tokyo. It is a global ecommerce personalization platform using machine learning and behavioral targeting to increase conversion rates. The company serves retail and ecommerce brands with real-time on-site personalization and AI-powered product recommendations.
Fanplayr.summary_abuse: Fanplayr collects detailed behavioral data about website visitors including clickstream data, browsing patterns, and purchase intent signals to power its personalization engine. This raises moderate privacy concerns. The platform uses first-party behavioral data for targeting, which is less invasive than cross-site tracking but still involves significant data collection. No major data breaches or regulatory violations have been publicly reported.

FaraPy.history: FaraPy was created in December 2020 by Mohammad Amin Ghaemi as an open-source Python CMS built on Django. It was released on PyPI under a BSD license with pip installation support. The project attracted minimal adoption (11 GitHub stars, 3 forks) and received its last code update in November 2021. No notable ownership changes, forks, or controversies.
FaraPy.summary_abuse: FaraPy is a niche, self-hosted Python CMS with no documented abuse. It has the theoretical capability to host arbitrary web content under custom domains, but its minimal adoption (11 GitHub stars) and abandonment since 2021 mean it has effectively zero abuse surface. No security research reports, known phishing incidents, or scam campaigns have been associated with it.

Farazi Bilişim.history: Farazi Bilişim is a Turkish SEO and digital marketing services company providing search engine optimization for businesses in Turkey.
Farazi Bilişim.summary_abuse: Farazi Bilişim is a niche Turkish SEO services platform with no documented abuse profile. Its limited geographic footprint and B2B service model pose minimal direct abuse risk.

FarCry.history: FarCry is an open-source web application framework for CFML (ColdFusion Markup Language) developers. Created in Australia around 2003, it was used to build content-managed websites. The framework is largely dormant with minimal recent activity as CFML usage declined.
FarCry.summary_abuse: FarCry is a niche CFML framework with very limited usage. As a self-hosted framework for a declining language ecosystem, it has no meaningful abuse surface. No notable incidents documented.

FareHarbor.history: FareHarbor was founded in 2013 in Hawaii by brothers Lawrence and Zachary Hester. It became the leading booking management platform for tours and activities. Booking Holdings (NASDAQ: BKNG) acquired FareHarbor in April 2018 in the largest deal in the tours-and-activities sector at the time. It now powers 20,000+ businesses globally.
FareHarbor.summary_abuse: FareHarbor is a tour and activity booking platform owned by Booking Holdings. As an established payment-processing platform for legitimate tourism businesses, its abuse risk is low. Standard risks include fraudulent chargebacks from tour operators. No major platform-level fraud incidents documented.

FARFETCH Black & White.history: Launched in 2015 as an independent business unit of FARFETCH, Black & White (later renamed FARFETCH Platform Solutions) offered a white-label enterprise ecommerce platform for luxury brands. Notable customers include Harrods, Manolo Blahnik, and Christopher Kane. FARFETCH acquired Stadium Goods and Browns Fashion to expand the platform ecosystem.
FARFETCH Black & White.summary_abuse: FARFETCH Black & White is an enterprise ecommerce platform for luxury fashion brands. As a B2B platform with enterprise-only contracts, it has minimal consumer-facing abuse surface. No notable phishing, fraud, or malware incidents documented.

Farmakom.history: Farmakom is an Italian SaaS platform serving pharmacy businesses with digital transformation tools including channel management and customer support features. Founded in Italy with a focus on the Italian pharmacy sector.
Farmakom.summary_abuse: Farmakom is a niche Italian platform serving pharmacies and has no documented abuse profile. It operates in a regulated pharmacy sector with legitimate B2B clients, presenting minimal abuse risk.

Faslet.history: Faslet is a Netherlands-based digital size advice company founded around 2020 by Robbert Hoogstraten and Ard Kortenoeven. It provides ML-powered size recommendation widgets for fashion ecommerce stores, available on Shopify, Magento, WooCommerce, and other platforms. Pricing starts at $199/month.
Faslet.summary_abuse: Faslet is a B2B size recommendation widget for fashion stores. It collects body measurements from shoppers which raises privacy considerations but the company is GDPR-compliant as a Netherlands company. No notable abuse or fraud incidents documented.

FASO.history: FASO (Fine Art Studio Online) is a niche website builder and marketing platform specifically designed for visual artists. It has operated for over 20 years helping artists sell their work online. The company rebranded its parent entity to BoldBrush. No major ownership changes or controversies documented.
FASO.summary_abuse: FASO is a specialized website builder for visual artists with subscription-based hosting. Given its niche artist community focus, low free-tier access, and art-selling purpose, it has minimal abuse profile. No notable phishing or fraud activity documented.

Fast Bundle.history: Fast Bundle is a Shopify app launched around 2020 that enables merchants to create product bundle offers with discounts to increase average order value. No notable ownership changes or controversies.
Fast Bundle.summary_abuse: Fast Bundle is a Shopify-integrated product bundling app with no documented abuse profile. Its function is merchant-side sales optimization and it has no consumer-facing hosting or file storage surface.

Fast Checkout.history: Fast Checkout was founded in 2019 by Domm Holland in San Francisco as a one-click checkout solution for e-commerce. The company raised over $120 million from investors including Stripe. Despite significant investment, the product failed to gain traction and Fast.co shut down in April 2022, laying off its entire staff.
Fast Checkout.summary_abuse: Fast Checkout was a legitimate one-click checkout service that shut down in 2022. During its operational period, it handled payment credentials, but there are no notable public abuse incidents. The service is now defunct.

FAST ESP.history: FAST ESP (Enterprise Search Platform) was introduced by Fast Search & Transfer in January 2004. Microsoft acquired Fast Search & Transfer for $1.2 billion in April 2008, gaining the FAST ESP product. The technology was integrated into Microsoft SharePoint's enterprise search capabilities and the standalone product was eventually deprecated.
FAST ESP.summary_abuse: FAST ESP is an enterprise search platform now owned by Microsoft. It is a backend indexing and search infrastructure with no consumer-facing surface and no documented abuse signal.

FAST Search for SharePoint.history: Microsoft acquired FAST Search & Transfer in 2008, integrating its technology into SharePoint as FAST Search Server 2010 for SharePoint. After SharePoint 2013 launched, Microsoft gradually deprecated FAST Search in favor of its unified SharePoint Search, which absorbed much of FAST's functionality. The product is no longer sold as a standalone offering.
FAST Search for SharePoint.summary_abuse: FAST Search for SharePoint is an on-premises enterprise search engine by Microsoft, running inside corporate SharePoint farms. As internal enterprise infrastructure, it has no consumer-facing surface and is not associated with any abuse patterns.

Fast Simon.history: Fast Simon (formerly InstantSearch+) is an AI-powered ecommerce search, merchandising, and personalization platform. It is a Certified Shopify Technology Partner and integrates with Shopify, BigCommerce, Magento and other platforms. No major ownership changes documented.
Fast Simon.summary_abuse: Fast Simon is an AI search and merchandising plugin for ecommerce platforms. As a B2B tool embedded in legitimate online stores, it has no notable consumer-facing abuse surface. No phishing or fraud incidents documented.

FastAPI.history: FastAPI was created by Colombian developer Sebastián Ramírez (tiangolo) and first released in December 2018. It quickly rose to become one of the most popular Python web frameworks, surpassing Django in GitHub stars. Built on Starlette and Pydantic, it delivers high performance comparable to NodeJS and Go. Ramírez was hired by Forethought to continue working on open source.
FastAPI.summary_abuse: FastAPI is a Python web framework used to build APIs. As a server-side framework, it has no consumer-facing surface and is not associated with abuse. Applications built on FastAPI could have vulnerabilities if improperly coded, but the framework itself has a strong security track record.

Fastbase.history: Fastbase is a visitor identification and lead generation analytics tool that de-anonymizes website visitors by linking IP addresses to company and individual data. Based in New Zealand, it operates in a space that raises privacy concerns due to its nature of identifying individuals browsing websites.
Fastbase.summary_abuse: Fastbase identifies website visitors by name and company, a capability that raises significant privacy concerns. The tool essentially de-anonymizes browsing behavior without individual consent, which may conflict with GDPR and other privacy regulations. While marketed for legitimate lead generation, the same capability can be misused for stalking or unauthorized profiling of individuals.

FastComet.history: FastComet was founded in 2013 as an independent web hosting company and quickly grew to serve over 750,000 website owners worldwide. The company operates 12 data centers globally in the Americas, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. In April 2025, World Host Group acquired FastComet, which continues to operate under its own brand while gaining access to World Host Group infrastructure and resources.
FastComet.summary_abuse: FastComet has no significant abuse history. It is a legitimate web hosting provider with a strong track record of customer service and uptime reliability. The 2025 acquisition by World Host Group has not introduced any known issues. Hosting providers may be used to host malicious content, but this is not a specific issue associated with FastComet beyond what is typical for shared hosting companies.

Fastcommerce.history: Fastcommerce is a Brazilian ecommerce platform founded around 2005, offering tools for businesses to create and manage online stores. It serves the Brazilian market with localized payment and logistics integrations.
Fastcommerce.summary_abuse: Fastcommerce is a Brazilian ecommerce SaaS platform with no documented significant abuse profile. Like any e-commerce hosting provider, it could theoretically host fake storefronts, but no notable incidents are publicly reported.

Fasterize.history: Fasterize is a French web performance and website acceleration company founded in 2011, based in Lyon. It provides a reverse-proxy-based acceleration service that optimizes front-end performance for e-commerce and media sites without requiring code changes.
Fasterize.summary_abuse: Fasterize is a web performance service operating as a reverse proxy for enterprise websites. It has no documented abuse profile as its service is used by vetted business clients, not end-users.

Fastly.history: Fastly was founded in 2011 by Artur Bergman and went public on the NYSE in 2019. It built a developer-focused CDN and edge cloud platform with over 60 global points of presence. Fastly pioneered real-time purging and edge computing on WebAssembly. Its customers include GitHub, Spotify, The New York Times, and Pinterest. In June 2021, a configuration bug caused a major global internet outage affecting hundreds of major websites.
Fastly.summary_abuse: Fastly is a trusted enterprise CDN and edge computing provider with no history of intentional abuse. The notable June 2021 global outage was caused by a software bug triggered by a customer configuration change, not malicious intent. Fastly maintains comprehensive security certifications including SOC2 Type II, ISO 27001, PCI-DSS, and HIPAA. It has faced criticism as a content delivery platform for occasionally hosting controversial content, but has content moderation policies.

Fastmind.history: Fastmind is an AI chatbot builder that uses live search engine data to power customer engagement conversations. Founded post-2022 as part of the generative AI wave.
Fastmind.summary_abuse: Fastmind is an AI chatbot platform with no significant documented abuse. As with any AI chatbot tool, there is a risk of being used to generate misleading automated responses, but no notable incidents are publicly recorded.

FastPixel.history: FastPixel is a cloud-based WordPress performance plugin created by the team behind ShortPixel, a Romanian image optimization company. Launched around 2022, it combines caching, image optimization via ShortPixel CDN, CSS/JS minification, and Core Web Vitals optimization in a single plugin.
FastPixel.summary_abuse: FastPixel is a WordPress performance plugin with CDN delivery. As a speed optimization tool for websites, it has no consumer-facing abuse surface. No notable phishing or fraud incidents documented.

FastSpring.history: FastSpring was founded in 2005 in Santa Barbara, California as a full-service ecommerce platform for software and SaaS companies. It acts as a Merchant of Record, handling payments, tax compliance, and subscription management. The company has grown steadily and serves thousands of software vendors globally.
FastSpring.summary_abuse: FastSpring serves as a Merchant of Record for software sales and has documented instances of fraudulent purchases and chargebacks, which is inherent to any payment platform. The company maintains fraud prevention controls and a compliance program. Its use in scam storefronts is limited compared to general-purpose platforms, as it focuses on software and SaaS.

Fastspring.history: FastSpring was founded in 2005 in Santa Barbara, California, as an ecommerce platform for software and digital goods vendors, handling global payments, subscriptions, and tax compliance. The company was acquired by Accel-KKR in a buyout and subsequently received a strategic investment from LLR Partners in 2024, indicating continued private equity backing.
Fastspring.summary_abuse: FastSpring is a legitimate software ecommerce platform with some documented exposure to abuse. Because it enables merchants to create checkout pages and sell digital goods, it has occasionally been used by fake software vendors or scam storefronts to process payments. FastSpring does enforce merchant vetting and has a compliance process, but like any payment-facilitation platform it has been implicated in occasional fraudulent merchant cases. Abuse is a small fraction of overall use and the vendor has compliance controls; legitimate software sales overwhelmingly dominate.

FastTony.history: FastTony is a social commerce tool that helps merchants promote and sell products through Facebook and Instagram advertising. Limited public history available.
FastTony.summary_abuse: FastTony facilitates Facebook and Instagram ad campaigns for merchants. As a social media marketing tool, its abuse risk is mainly tied to potential use for misleading ad campaigns; however, no significant public abuse incidents are documented.

Fat Zebra.history: Fat Zebra is an Australian payment gateway founded in 2011, providing online credit card processing to businesses in Australia. It is one of the few independent Australian payment gateways focused on the local market.
Fat Zebra.summary_abuse: Fat Zebra is a PCI-DSS compliant payment gateway serving Australian businesses. As a payments processor, it is subject to the same fraud risks as any payment platform, but its focused Australian B2B market and compliance posture limit abuse exposure. No notable public incidents are documented.

Fat-Free Framework.history: Fat-Free Framework (F3) is a PHP micro-framework created by Bong Cosca in 2009. It emphasizes simplicity and minimal configuration, providing routing, ORM, caching, and other features in a single file. The project is maintained on GitHub and has a small but dedicated community.
Fat-Free Framework.summary_abuse: Fat-Free Framework is an open-source PHP micro-framework with no consumer-facing surface. It has no meaningful abuse profile as it is a developer tool used to build server-side web applications.

FatherShops.history: FatherShops is a small ecommerce platform with limited public documentation. It provides basic online store functionality for small businesses.
FatherShops.summary_abuse: FatherShops is a minor ecommerce platform with no documented abuse profile. As an online store hosting service, it carries typical ecommerce platform risk but no notable public incidents are recorded.

Fathom.history: Fathom Analytics was founded in 2018 by Jack Ellis and Paul Jarvis in Canada as a privacy-first alternative to Google Analytics. It counts page views without using cookies or tracking personal data, making it fully GDPR, CCPA, and PECR compliant by design. The founders have been vocal advocates of privacy-respecting web analytics.
Fathom.summary_abuse: Fathom Analytics is a privacy-focused web analytics service that does not track individuals or store personal data. It has no documented abuse profile. Its privacy-by-design architecture means it does not accumulate personally identifiable data that could be misused.

Fatsoma.history: Fatsoma is a UK-based event ticketing platform founded around 2010, focused on live events, concerts, and club nights. It allows event organizers to list events and sell tickets directly.
Fatsoma.summary_abuse: Fatsoma is an event ticketing platform that could theoretically host listings for fake events. However, as a niche UK platform serving vetted event organizers, documented abuse is minimal. The company maintains standard UK consumer protection compliance.

Faveo.history: Faveo Helpdesk is an open-source help desk and ticketing system founded in 2016 by Ladybird Web Solution in India. It offers both a free community edition and paid hosted plans, making it a popular self-hosted alternative to commercial ticketing tools.
Faveo.summary_abuse: Faveo Helpdesk is a self-hosted help desk system with no meaningful abuse profile from an end-user perspective. As a business tool for managing support tickets, it processes customer data but has no public record of systematic misuse.

Fbits.history: Fbits is a Brazilian ecommerce platform that became part of Tray Corp, a Brazilian ecommerce technology group. It serves the Brazilian market with tools for online store management and sales.
Fbits.summary_abuse: Fbits is a Brazilian ecommerce platform under Tray Corp with no documented abuse profile. It serves B2B clients in the Brazilian ecommerce market.

FC2 Analyzer.history: FC2 was established in July 1999 in Las Vegas, Nevada as a US corporation providing web services primarily for the Japanese market, including blog hosting, website builder, and analytics tools. FC2 Analyzer was a free web analytics and counter service that officially ended on June 2, 2025.
FC2 Analyzer.summary_abuse: FC2 is a Japanese-market web portal with a history of hosting adult and user-generated content that has been associated with copyright violations and adult content abuse. The FC2 Analyzer tracking service itself is now deprecated. The FC2 platform more broadly has been investigated by Japanese authorities for obscene content distribution.

FeatherPanel.history: FeatherPanel is an open-source game server management panel built with PHP, Vue, and Go. It is a newer community project with limited public history.
FeatherPanel.summary_abuse: FeatherPanel is an open-source self-hosted game server management tool. As a self-hosted solution with no centralized user hosting, it presents no direct consumer-facing abuse surface to third parties.

FeatherX.history: FeatherX is an AI-powered review aggregation and social listening tool that captures content from major review platforms and social media. A newer entrant in the reputation management space.
FeatherX.summary_abuse: FeatherX is a review and social media aggregation tool. It collects public content from review platforms and social channels for analysis, with no notable abuse profile documented.

Feathr.history: Feathr is a US-based digital marketing platform founded around 2013, specifically designed for event organizers, nonprofits, and associations. It provides tools for targeted digital advertising and audience engagement tied to events.
Feathr.summary_abuse: Feathr is a marketing automation platform for event organizers with no documented abuse profile. Its targeted ad delivery for events presents minimal abuse risk beyond standard digital advertising concerns.

Featurebase.history: Featurebase is a product feedback and feature management platform founded in 2021 in Estonia. It helps product teams collect and prioritize user feedback through public roadmaps and voting boards.
Featurebase.summary_abuse: Featurebase is a product feedback management tool with no documented abuse profile. It is used by SaaS companies to manage feature requests and does not host user-generated content in a way that creates significant abuse risk.

FedEx.history: FedEx was founded in 1971 by Frederick W. Smith in Memphis, Tennessee. Originally Federal Express, it pioneered overnight package delivery and became one of the world's largest courier companies. The company went public in 1978 and has grown through numerous acquisitions including Kinko's (2004) and TNT Express (2016).
FedEx.summary_abuse: FedEx is widely impersonated in phishing campaigns and package delivery scam texts and emails. Fraudsters regularly send fake FedEx tracking notifications to harvest credentials or install malware. However, FedEx as a legitimate company is not itself responsible for this abuse, and it actively works with law enforcement to counter such impersonation. The APIs themselves are not significantly abused.

Fedora.history: Fedora is a free open-source Linux distribution sponsored by Red Hat, first released in 2003 as a successor to the original Red Hat Linux. It serves as a community-driven upstream for Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL). When IBM acquired Red Hat in 2019 for $34 billion, Fedora's governance model and community remained largely intact.
Fedora.summary_abuse: Fedora is a mainstream Linux distribution with no meaningful direct abuse profile. As a general-purpose operating system, it can be installed on any hardware including servers used for various purposes, but no specific abuse patterns are attributed to Fedora over other Linux distributions.

Feedback Fish.history: Feedback Fish is a simple feedback widget for websites, built by a solo developer around 2020. It targets small businesses and developers who want an easy way to collect user feedback without complex survey tools.
Feedback Fish.summary_abuse: Feedback Fish is a minimal feedback widget with no documented abuse profile. As a simple data collection widget with no user-generated hosting, it presents no meaningful abuse risk.

FeedbackAutomatic.history: FeedbackAutomatic is a customer feedback and online review generation platform aimed at helping businesses collect surveys and boost their reputation on review sites. Limited public history available.
FeedbackAutomatic.summary_abuse: FeedbackAutomatic facilitates online review generation campaigns, which is a practice that can edge into incentivized or manipulated reviews if not properly controlled. No specific abuse incidents are documented, but the category carries inherent risks around fake review generation.

FeederNinja.history: FeederNinja is an RSS and social media feed widget tool that allows website owners to create customizable feed embeds. Limited public history is available.
FeederNinja.summary_abuse: FeederNinja is a feed widget tool with no documented abuse profile. Its widget-based content aggregation model presents minimal abuse risk.

Feedify.history: Feedify is a customer engagement automation platform offering push notifications, live chat, and feed management tools. Founded around 2017, it targets small to medium businesses.
Feedify.summary_abuse: Feedify is a customer engagement tool offering push notifications and live chat. Push notification services can be abused for spam, though no notable incidents involving Feedify specifically are documented.

FeedMagnet.history: FeedMagnet is a content curation platform that aggregates original content with curated third-party articles and social posts. Founded around 2012, it targets brands needing mixed-content experiences.
FeedMagnet.summary_abuse: FeedMagnet is a content curation tool with no documented significant abuse profile. Its content aggregation function is B2B-focused and presents minimal direct consumer abuse risk.

Feednami.history: Feednami is a web service by Sekando that was created as a free replacement for the discontinued Google Feed API, allowing web applications to access and parse RSS and Atom feeds via a simple API call.
Feednami.summary_abuse: Feednami is an RSS feed proxy service with no documented abuse profile. It is a utility that fetches public RSS feeds and is used by developers as a Google Feed API replacement.

Feedonomics.history: Feedonomics was founded in 2014 by Brian Roizen and Shawn Lipman in Los Angeles as a product feed management platform for ecommerce brands. It was acquired by BigCommerce in 2021, becoming their product feed management solution while continuing to operate independently.
Feedonomics.summary_abuse: Feedonomics is a B2B product feed management platform for ecommerce retailers with no documented abuse profile. Its enterprise-focused model and acquisition by BigCommerce further limit abuse risk.

FeedSpring.history: FeedSpring is a social media feed widget tool that allows website owners to embed styled social media feeds on their sites. Limited public history is available.
FeedSpring.summary_abuse: FeedSpring embeds social media content on websites and has no documented abuse profile. Its widget-based model presents no direct user-facing abuse risk.

Feefo.history: Feefo was founded in 2006 and is headquartered in the UK with offices in the US and Australia. It is a verified reviews platform that collects authenticated customer feedback by only inviting customers who have completed a transaction. The company employs over 150 people and serves thousands of clients worldwide. It positions itself as the world's largest verified reviews platform focused on transaction-linked social proof.
Feefo.summary_abuse: Feefo has no significant abuse history. It is a legitimate B2B reviews collection platform that operates on a permission basis, only reaching customers who have completed verified transactions. The platform does not engage in cross-site tracking and collects data in accordance with GDPR. No major security incidents or privacy violations have been reported.

Fello.history: Fello is a real estate seller lead generation platform designed specifically for real estate agents, providing lead capture and nurturing tools to help agents identify and retain potential home sellers.
Fello.summary_abuse: Fello is a real estate lead generation tool targeted at licensed agents. Its B2B professional services model limits direct consumer abuse risk, and no notable incidents are documented.

Felmat.history: Felmat is a performance advertising network that connects advertisers with publishers for cost-per-action and affiliate marketing campaigns. It operates in the ad-tech space with limited public history.
Felmat.summary_abuse: Felmat is a performance advertising network operating in the affiliate and CPA marketing space. Ad networks of this type are occasionally implicated in serving deceptive ads or low-quality advertising inventory. No specific major incidents are documented, but the category carries elevated abuse risk due to the open publisher recruitment model.

Fenicio.history: Fenicio is a Spanish cloud ecommerce platform focused on managing omnichannel sales operations. It provides tools for product catalog, inventory, and order management across multiple sales channels.
Fenicio.summary_abuse: Fenicio is a Spanish ecommerce operations platform with no documented abuse profile. It serves B2B retail clients in the Spanish market.

Fera.history: Fera.ai is a Canadian social proof and product review platform founded in 2017. It helps ecommerce stores collect and display product reviews, ratings, and social proof widgets to increase conversion rates.
Fera.summary_abuse: Fera is a review and social proof tool for ecommerce. Review platforms carry inherent risk of fake review generation, but Fera verifies purchases before allowing reviews. No notable abuse incidents are documented.

Fera Product Reviews App.history: Fera Product Reviews App is the Shopify-specific version of Fera.ai, a Canadian review and social proof platform founded in 2017. It integrates directly with Shopify stores to collect and display verified product reviews.
Fera Product Reviews App.summary_abuse: Fera Product Reviews App is a Shopify review tool that verifies purchases before allowing reviews. This reduces but does not eliminate fake review risk. No notable abuse incidents are documented.

Feroot.history: Feroot is a Canadian payment security platform founded in 2018, specializing in PCI DSS 4.0 compliant payment page protection. It monitors and secures checkout forms against client-side attacks like web skimming and Magecart.
Feroot.summary_abuse: Feroot is a security tool that helps organizations defend payment pages against web skimming attacks. It is used by enterprises to prevent breaches rather than facilitate them. No abuse incidents are documented.

Ferret One.history: Ferret One is a Japanese website and landing page builder designed for business marketing, particularly popular in the Japanese market. It provides no-code tools for creating marketing pages.
Ferret One.summary_abuse: Ferret One is a Japanese website builder with no documented significant abuse profile. It targets B2B clients in Japan for marketing page creation.

Ferron.history: Ferron is a fast, memory-safe web server written in Rust, rebranded from Project Karpacz. It was announced on Hacker News in 2025 where it received community attention. Built on Tokio and Hyper async Rust libraries, it features automatic SSL/TLS with Let's Encrypt, modular architecture, reverse proxy with load balancing, and FastCGI support. It is licensed under MIT and remains an early-stage open source project.
Ferron.summary_abuse: Ferron has no known abuse history. It is a new, lightweight open-source web server focused on security and performance through Rust memory safety guarantees. As an MIT-licensed project with full source availability, it is fully auditable. Being very new, its security track record is limited but no vulnerabilities or malicious behavior have been reported.

Fever.history: Fever was founded in 2014 by Pep Gomez in Barcelona, Spain, as a live-entertainment discovery and ticketing platform. The company raised $227 million led by Goldman Sachs in 2022, reached a $1 billion valuation, and raised a further $110 million in 2023 at a $1.8 billion valuation. By 2025 the platform reported 200 million monthly users across 40 countries and $724 million in annual revenue, cementing its position as the leading independent live entertainment tech platform.
Fever.summary_abuse: Fever is a consumer ticketing and event discovery platform with no notable abuse signal relative to its category. As a legitimate entertainment marketplace operating in 40 countries, its primary risk profile is consumer-facing ticket fraud where third parties may resell fake tickets obtained via the platform, rather than Fever itself enabling abuse. The platform collects behavioral and location data to personalize recommendations, which raises routine privacy considerations but not significant abuse concerns.

FiboSearch.history: FiboSearch is a Polish WooCommerce product search plugin, formerly known as AJAX Search for WooCommerce. It was developed as an enhanced search solution for WooCommerce stores with instant search results and product thumbnails.
FiboSearch.summary_abuse: FiboSearch is a self-hosted WooCommerce search plugin with no consumer-facing surface outside the context of the stores that install it. No abuse profile is documented.

Fider.history: Fider is an open-source customer feedback and feature voting platform released in 2017. It is built with Go and React and is available for self-hosting under AGPL-3.0 or as a managed cloud service.
Fider.summary_abuse: Fider is an open-source feedback collection platform with no documented abuse profile. As a product feedback tool, it has no significant consumer-facing abuse surface.

Fieldd.history: Fieldd is an Australian field service management and scheduling software founded around 2018. It helps businesses plan, assign, and manage field service operations including scheduling and dispatch.
Fieldd.summary_abuse: Fieldd is a field service management tool with no documented abuse profile. Its B2B operational management focus presents no direct consumer abuse risk.

FieldStack Omni.history: FieldStack Omni is a unified commerce platform for retail businesses that combines web store, third-party channel management, and point-of-sale operations in a single system. It is targeted at specialty retailers.
FieldStack Omni.summary_abuse: FieldStack Omni is an enterprise retail management platform with no documented abuse profile. Its B2B retail focus and enterprise sales model present minimal abuse risk.

Filamentphp.history: Filament is a PHP/Laravel admin panel and form-building library created by Dan Harrin and Ryan Chandler, first released in 2021. It rapidly gained popularity in the Laravel ecosystem for building backend admin interfaces and full-stack TALL-stack applications.
Filamentphp.summary_abuse: Filament is an open-source Laravel library for building admin panels with no consumer-facing surface. It has no abuse profile.

Fileflare.history: Fileflare is a Shopify digital downloads app that enables merchants to sell and automatically deliver downloadable digital products to customers after purchase.
Fileflare.summary_abuse: Fileflare is a digital downloads delivery app for Shopify. Like all digital distribution platforms, it could be used to sell pirated content, though no notable incidents are documented and it targets legitimate Shopify merchants.

FileHippo.history: FileHippo is a UK-based software download portal founded around 2004. It provides a catalog of software downloads and an update checker widget. It has been consistently popular as a free software download source but has faced criticism for bundling adware and PUPs with some downloads.
FileHippo.summary_abuse: FileHippo has historically been associated with distributing software bundled with adware and potentially unwanted programs (PUPs). Security researchers and users have reported receiving unexpected software alongside legitimate downloads from the site. The update checker widget also collects installed software inventory. The site uses heavy advertising and download buttons that can mislead users into downloading incorrect files.

FilePond.history: FilePond is an open-source JavaScript file-upload library created by Rik Schennink under the PQINA brand, first released around 2018. The library gained popularity for its smooth animations, image optimization capabilities, and accessibility focus, accumulating over 15,700 GitHub stars. It supports React, Vue, Angular, Svelte, and jQuery adapters and is maintained independently by its original author with no commercial backing.
FilePond.summary_abuse: FilePond is a client-side JavaScript library for file uploads with no server-side component and no consumer-facing surface. There is no documented abuse signal; it is a frontend developer tool that simply provides UI for file uploads. Abuse potential depends entirely on the server-side implementation of the applications using it, not on the library itself.

Filestack.history: Filestack was founded in 2011 (originally as Filepicker.io) in San Francisco. It provides file upload, transformation, and delivery APIs. The company rebranded from Filepicker to Filestack in 2015 to better reflect its expanded feature set beyond just file picking.
Filestack.summary_abuse: Filestack is a file upload and storage API platform that processes user files. As a general-purpose file handling service, malicious files could theoretically be uploaded through applications built on Filestack, but the platform itself has content scanning capabilities and is not specifically known for abuse. No major public incidents are documented.

Fillout.history: Fillout is a US-based form builder platform founded in 2022 that offers advanced form building with integrations to Notion, Airtable, and Salesforce. It quickly gained traction in the no-code/low-code market.
Fillout.summary_abuse: Fillout is a form builder that collects user-submitted data on behalf of businesses. Form builders can be used to create phishing forms, but Fillout's verified customer model and terms of service enforcement limit this risk. No significant abuse incidents are documented.

Finalsite.history: Finalsite was founded in 1999 as a web design and CMS platform specifically for K-12 schools. It is one of the leading school website platforms in the US, serving thousands of public and private schools. The company experienced a notable ransomware attack in January 2022 that disrupted hundreds of school websites.
Finalsite.summary_abuse: Finalsite experienced a significant ransomware attack in January 2022 that took hundreds of K-12 school websites offline for several days. As a school website platform, it hosts sensitive information about minors. The incident highlighted the importance of cybersecurity in the education technology sector.

Finalytics.history: Finalytics is an AI-powered customer experience analytics platform. It uses data analytics to help businesses transform their customer experience through personalization. Limited public history available.
Finalytics.summary_abuse: Finalytics is a customer analytics platform with no documented abuse profile. It processes customer behavioral data for business insights.

FinanceAds.history: FinanceAds is a German affiliate marketing network specialized in the financial sector, founded in 2009. It connects financial product advertisers (banks, insurance companies, lending platforms) with publisher affiliates who promote financial products on a performance basis.
FinanceAds.summary_abuse: FinanceAds is a financial sector affiliate network operating in an inherently risk-prone niche. Financial affiliate programs are frequently implicated in misleading advertising of loans, credit cards, and insurance products. The financial services sector sees above-average rates of deceptive affiliate campaigns. FinanceAds operates under German regulatory oversight which provides some protection, but monitoring the full publisher network for compliant advertising practices is challenging.

FinConnect.history: FinConnect is a Dutch platform that enables financial transactions and connections between individuals and businesses, supporting money management and access to financial services in the Netherlands.
FinConnect.summary_abuse: FinConnect is a Dutch financial services platform operating in a regulated financial environment. As a financial services provider, it is subject to Dutch financial regulation and no notable abuse incidents are documented.

Findbar.history: Findbar is an AI-powered product search and discovery platform for e-commerce websites. It was launched around 2020 as a SaaS focused on improving on-site search results and conversion. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes documented.
Findbar.summary_abuse: Findbar is a niche e-commerce search SaaS with no documented abuse history. As a backend search integration tool, it has minimal consumer-facing surface area. No notable misuse patterns or abuse reports have been identified.

Findberry.history: Findberry is a free internal site search service that lets webmasters add an ad-free search engine to their websites without programming knowledge. It has fewer than 300 known customers and is run by a small or solo team. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes documented.
Findberry.summary_abuse: Findberry is a niche site-search SaaS with no documented abuse history. As an embedded internal search widget, it has minimal consumer-facing attack surface. No evidence of misuse or notable security incidents.

FindGore.history: FindGore is a UK-based conversion rate optimization analytics tool focused on analyzing user behavior on websites to improve conversion rates. It operates as a small independent SaaS with a very limited install base. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes documented.
FindGore.summary_abuse: FindGore is a niche analytics SaaS with no documented abuse history. It operates as a backend analytics tool embedded on websites with no notable consumer-facing surface that would attract abuse. The service collects behavioral data for site owners which carries typical privacy considerations but no documented misuse patterns.

Findify.history: Findify was founded in Sweden around 2014 as an AI-driven e-commerce search and personalization platform. The company gained traction integrating with Shopify and other platforms. It was later acquired by Maropost, a Canadian marketing software company, and operates as part of the Maropost product suite. No major controversies documented.
Findify.summary_abuse: Findify is an e-commerce search and personalization SaaS with no documented abuse history. It processes shopper behavioral data to personalize results, which carries standard privacy considerations. As a backend integration tool, it has no notable consumer-facing abuse surface.

Findigs.history: Findigs is a US-based AI-native tenant screening platform for property managers, founded around 2019. It provides automated rental application processing, identity and income verification, and fraud detection. The platform is FCRA-compliant. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes documented.
Findigs.summary_abuse: Findigs handles sensitive personal data including identity documents, income records, and credit information for tenant screening. It is FCRA-compliant and includes automated fraud detection to flag manipulated documents. As a property management B2B tool, it is not a significant abuse vector; the primary risk is data security given the sensitivity of PII processed.

Findmeashoe.history: Findmeashoe is a virtual shoe fitting SaaS that uses AI and 3D technology to recommend shoe sizes from foot images. The company launched around 2017 and has served footwear retailers and manufacturers. It has delivered over a million recommendations and claims to reduce returns by 20-60%. No notable acquisitions documented.
Findmeashoe.summary_abuse: Findmeashoe is a niche footwear-fitting SaaS with no documented abuse history. The service processes foot images and measurements to recommend shoe sizes, which involves some biometric data collection, but operates in a narrow B2B footwear vertical with no notable consumer-facing abuse vectors.

Fing.history: Fing was developed as a network scanner app with patented device recognition technology. It became one of the leading network scanning tools with over 500 million uniquely identified devices. In October 2020, Lansweeper, an IT asset management platform, acquired Fing. The company has offices in London, Dublin, Sydney, and Rome.
Fing.summary_abuse: Fing is a legitimate network discovery and security tool used by consumers and IT professionals to scan local networks. It has no notable history of abuse as a platform. The tool itself could theoretically be used for unauthorized network scanning, but the vendor targets legitimate home and business users and the app is widely trusted. No documented cases of misuse at scale.

FingerprintJS.history: FingerprintJS was created by Valentin Vasilyev around 2012 as an open-source browser fingerprinting library. Fingerprint Inc. was founded around 2019 to commercialize the technology. The open-source library was relicensed from MIT to BSL in 2021, limiting production commercial use without a paid subscription, which caused notable community backlash.
FingerprintJS.summary_abuse: FingerprintJS is primarily marketed for fraud prevention and bot detection, but browser fingerprinting can also enable cross-site tracking without user consent. Privacy advocates flag it as privacy-invasive since fingerprints persist across incognito sessions and cookie clears. The library is widely embedded on commercial websites. The 2021 BSL relicensing created controversy around its open-source claims.

Finsweet.history: Finsweet is a Webflow-focused development agency that created the widely-used Attributes open-source JavaScript library for Webflow. Their Attributes library serves over half a billion page views per month and is a de facto standard in the Webflow ecosystem. The company also produces Client-First conventions and the Wized web app framework.
Finsweet.summary_abuse: Finsweet Attributes has no documented abuse history. It is a legitimate developer tooling library used by Webflow agencies and freelancers globally. No notable security incidents or misuse patterns have been documented.

FintechOS.history: FintechOS was founded in 2017 by Sergiu Negut and Teo Blidarus in Bucharest, Romania. The company built a financial product lifecycle management platform used by banks and insurers including Societe Generale and Admiral Insurance. It raised $146M total across multiple rounds, including a $60M Series B+ from BlackRock, Cipio Partners, and Molten Ventures in May 2024. The company is co-headquartered in London and New York.
FintechOS.summary_abuse: FintechOS is a B2B enterprise platform for financial institutions with no documented abuse history. As a backend platform serving regulated banks and insurers, it has minimal consumer-facing attack surface. The company operates in a heavily regulated industry and enforces strict compliance requirements for customers.

FireApps Ali Reviews.history: Founded in 2016 in Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam as FireApps JSC. Ali Reviews was the company's first Shopify app, later rebranded to Kudosi. The company grew to serve over 40,000 Shopify merchants and operates under the FireGroup Technology brand. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes identified.
FireApps Ali Reviews.summary_abuse: FireApps Ali Reviews is a Shopify review import app used heavily in the dropshipping ecosystem and has a moderate abuse signal. It is commonly used to import bulk fabricated or scraped AliExpress reviews onto dropshipping storefronts, inflating perceived product credibility. This practice, while not unique to this app, is a known pattern in low-quality dropshipping stores that may constitute consumer deception. Legitimate use for genuine review collection is widespread, but the app's AliExpress import feature specifically enables fake review population. No documented security incidents or malware associations identified.

Firebase.history: Firebase evolved from Envolve, a startup founded by James Tamplin and Andrew Lee in 2011. They separated the real-time data architecture into Firebase in April 2012. Google acquired Firebase in October 2014 and at Google I/O 2016 expanded it into a unified backend-as-a-service platform including analytics, authentication, hosting, cloud functions, and Firestore database.
Firebase.summary_abuse: Firebase's generous free tier and easy setup make it an occasional target for abuse including phishing page hosting, malware distribution, and spam relay. Firebase Hosting has been used to host phishing kits mimicking legitimate services because the firebase.app domain appears trustworthy. Google actively combats abuse with automated detection and takedown policies, but the free tier with custom domain support creates ongoing abuse pressure typical for large hosting platforms.

Fireblade.history: Fireblade was founded in 2008 in Israel by Shay Rapaport and Erez Azaria. The company developed a cloud-based bot management and behavioral web application firewall with CDN capabilities. It raised $4.5M in a Series A in 2013. The company was subsequently acquired by StackPath, a US CDN and edge security provider.
Fireblade.summary_abuse: Fireblade is a web security and CDN platform focused on protecting websites from bots, DDoS, and scraping. As a security product, its main exposure is as legitimate infrastructure. No documented abuse of the platform itself as a vector for harm.

Firefish Software.history: Firefish Software was founded in 2010 in Glasgow, Scotland by Wendy McDougall, who had previously run a top-billing recruitment agency. The company built a combined CRM, ATS, and marketing platform for recruitment agencies. It has been named in the top 100 fastest growing UK tech companies for four consecutive years and secured a £4m strategic investment. The company supports agencies in over 25 countries.
Firefish Software.summary_abuse: Firefish Software is a recruitment CRM and ATS platform with no documented abuse history. As a B2B software focused on staffing agencies, it handles candidate PII but has no significant consumer-facing attack surface. No notable security incidents or misuse patterns documented.

Firefly Reservations.history: Firefly Reservations is a cloud-based campground and RV park reservation management software. Founded in the mid-2010s in the US, it provides online booking, interactive maps, POS, and automated payment processing for campground operators. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes documented.
Firefly Reservations.summary_abuse: Firefly Reservations is a niche campground management SaaS with no documented abuse history. It processes reservation and payment data for outdoor hospitality businesses but has no notable consumer-facing attack surface beyond standard booking transactions.

Firepush.history: Firepush is a Shopify marketing app for SMS and web push notifications, launched around 2017. The app has grown to serve over 20,000 Shopify merchants across 130+ countries. It focuses on abandoned cart recovery and promotional campaigns. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes documented.
Firepush.summary_abuse: Firepush is a push notification and SMS marketing platform for Shopify stores. Push notification services can be misused for spam, and unsolicited marketing campaigns are an occasional concern. However, Firepush operates within the Shopify ecosystem which enforces its own AUP. The platform itself is not known as a significant abuse vector.

Fireside.history: Fireside.fm is a podcast hosting and analytics platform founded by Dan Benjamin, a professional podcaster who created it to address limitations in existing hosting tools. The platform offers RSS-based distribution to all major podcast directories. Notable co-founders include Mark Cuban and Falon Fatemi. The service established a partnership with Liberated Syndication (Libsyn) in 2021.
Fireside.summary_abuse: Fireside.fm is a podcast hosting platform with no documented significant abuse history. Like all podcast hosting services, the platform could theoretically host misleading or harmful audio content, but the service primarily serves independent creators with a clean reputation in the podcasting community.

Firework.history: Firework was founded in 2017 by Vincent Yang and Jerry Luk in San Mateo, California, as a video commerce platform enabling brands to embed shoppable and interactive video experiences on their websites. The company has raised over $250 million in total funding and partners with over 1,500 brands globally across beauty, fashion, and grocery.
Firework.summary_abuse: Firework is a video commerce platform for brands with no documented abuse history. As a premium B2B service with enterprise clients, it has a low risk profile for direct abuse. The platform embeds video on retail websites which carries standard content moderation responsibilities.

FirstHive.history: FirstHive was founded in 2016 in Bangalore, India. The company developed a Customer Data Platform that uses machine learning to build unified customer identities from multiple data sources. It has raised $9.13M across 8 rounds from 33 investors including Mela Ventures. The platform serves enterprise clients across the US, India, and ASEAN regions.
FirstHive.summary_abuse: FirstHive is a B2B customer data platform aggregating first-party marketing data for enterprises. The platform collects and unifies customer behavioral data across touchpoints which carries standard privacy considerations for CDP solutions. No documented abuse incidents or misuse patterns.

FirstImpression.io.history: FirstImpression.io is an ad monetization platform founded in Tel Aviv, Israel, around 2015. The company helps publishers monetize display, video, and native ad inventory through a single JavaScript tag. It uses big data analytics and automated A/B testing for yield optimization.
FirstImpression.io.summary_abuse: FirstImpression.io is an ad tech monetization platform that injects third-party ad scripts into publisher pages. Ad tech platforms are a known vector for malvertising campaigns where fraudulent advertisers serve malware or phishing content through legitimate ad networks. The platform deploys heavy tracking scripts and operates as a third-party ad intermediary, which increases the risk of malvertising incidents common across the industry.

FirstPromoter.history: FirstPromoter was founded in 2017 and positions itself as an all-in-one affiliate and referral tracking platform built specifically for SaaS companies. It integrates with Stripe, Paddle, and other billing platforms. The company is bootstrapped and primarily serves subscription SaaS businesses looking to launch partner programs.
FirstPromoter.summary_abuse: FirstPromoter is an affiliate tracking platform with built-in fraud protection features. Affiliate marketing platforms can be exploited for commission fraud and fake referrals, but FirstPromoter includes automated fraud checks. No notable security incidents or misuse patterns beyond standard affiliate program fraud risks.

Fit Analytics.history: Fit Analytics was founded around 2010 in Berlin, Germany as an apparel size recommendation engine using machine learning and shopper body data. It was acquired by Snap Inc. (Snapchat's parent company) as part of Snap's push into AR shopping and virtual try-on experiences.
Fit Analytics.summary_abuse: Fit Analytics collects shopper body measurement data for size recommendations. The platform processes personal biometric information (body measurements, fit preferences) which carries privacy considerations. As a B2B retailer integration, it has no significant direct abuse surface.

Fitizzy.history: Fitizzy is a French fashion-tech startup providing AI-powered size recommendation and virtual fitting solutions for e-commerce retailers. The platform generates morphological passports for customers to help reduce clothing returns. Clients include Cyrillus, Promod, Etam, and Celio. The company focuses on ready-to-wear for men, women, kids, and babies as well as lingerie and shoes.
Fitizzy.summary_abuse: No notable abuse or security incidents identified. Fitizzy is a niche B2B SaaS tool for fashion retailers focused on size recommendation. GDPR compliance is expected as a French company. No history of data misuse reported.

Fitle.history: Fitle is a French AI-powered size recommendation SaaS for e-commerce fashion retailers. Using machine learning the platform recommends clothing sizes based on body measurements with over 96% accuracy. Results include 4-6% conversion improvement and 40% returns reduction. It integrates into e-commerce platforms as a widget for online shoppers.
Fitle.summary_abuse: No notable abuse or security incidents identified. Fitle is a niche B2B SaaS for fashion e-commerce. As a French company it is subject to GDPR. No history of data misuse or privacy violations reported.

Fitssey.history: Fitssey is an all-in-one fitness studio management platform offering scheduling, booking, payments, and client management. It targets yoga, pilates, crossfit, dance, and general fitness studios. The platform provides 24/7 self-service booking, smart waitlists, Google and Apple Pay support, membership management, and smart lock integration. Pricing starts at EUR 9/month with a free tier for studios under 50 bookings/month.
Fitssey.summary_abuse: No abuse or security incidents identified. Fitssey is a legitimate fitness studio management SaaS serving small fitness businesses. As a European company it operates under GDPR. No data misuse or deceptive practices reported.

Fittingbox.history: Fittingbox is a French company founded around 2008 and recognized as the pioneer and inventor of virtual try-on technology for eyewear. Based in Toulouse with a US office in Miami, it serves over 4,000 clients worldwide including major eyewear brands and opticians. The platform offers augmented reality virtual try-on for over 195,000 eyeglass and sunglass models, serving 10 million users monthly. The Shopify app extends reach to e-commerce merchants.
Fittingbox.summary_abuse: No abuse or security incidents identified. Fittingbox is a legitimate B2B SaaS platform for eyewear virtual try-on with a long track record. As a French company operating globally it adheres to GDPR. No privacy violations or data misuse incidents reported.

FitVids.JS.history: FitVids.js was created by Dave Rupert and Chris Coyier around 2012 as a lightweight jQuery plugin to make embedded video iframes responsive. It became widely adopted in responsive web design before CSS aspect-ratio support made it largely unnecessary. The project has been dormant as native CSS solutions replaced it.
FitVids.JS.summary_abuse: FitVids.js is a dormant client-side video responsiveness library with no data collection or server components. It has no abuse surface.

Five9.history: Five9 was founded on December 20, 2001 by Alec Miloslavsky in San Ramon, California, with a mission to virtualize call center functions via the cloud. The name references 99.999% uptime. The company pioneered VoIP and SaaS-based contact center software. CEO Mike Burkland joined in 2008 and scaled it from SMB to enterprise. Five9 went public on NASDAQ (FIVN) in 2014. A proposed $14.7B acquisition by Zoom in 2021 was terminated. Revenue reached $1.04 billion in 2024 with over 3,000 clients and 2,600 employees.
Five9.summary_abuse: No abuse or major security incidents identified. Five9 is a publicly traded enterprise CCaaS provider with strong compliance certifications including SOC2, HIPAA, PCI DSS, and ISO 27001. No significant data breach or misconduct history reported.

Fixel.history: Fixel is an Israeli AI-powered audience segmentation tool for digital marketers. By adding a single JavaScript snippet, the platform scores website visitors based on engagement level in real time and automatically creates remarketing audiences. It integrates with Google Ads, Facebook Ads, and LinkedIn for automated targeting. The platform targets campaign managers seeking higher-quality audiences without manual segmentation.
Fixel.summary_abuse: Fixel operates as a behavioral tracking and audience segmentation tool, which involves collecting visitor engagement data. This is standard in the adtech industry. No specific abuse incidents identified, though the nature of the product involves cross-site audience data. GDPR compliance is claimed. No significant complaints or regulatory actions found.

Fixer.history: Fixer.io is a foreign exchange rate and currency conversion API service providing real-time and historical data for over 170 currencies. Data is sourced from 15+ financial providers including the European Central Bank and updated every 60 minutes. The free tier allows 1,000 requests per month with EUR as base currency. Historical data goes back to January 1, 1999. Widely used by developers, SMBs, and enterprises for currency conversion needs.
Fixer.summary_abuse: No abuse or security incidents identified. Fixer.io is a straightforward financial data API with transparent pricing. Connections are encrypted with 256-bit SSL. No incidents of data manipulation or API abuse reported. The free tier has reasonable rate limits to prevent misuse.

Flable.history: Flable is a German AI-powered digital marketing analytics platform founded in 2023 by Anirudh Saraiya, based in Munich. The platform unifies and correlates marketing data to provide insights for D2C brands. It serves as a digital marketing co-pilot offering AI-driven marketing strategy, SEO optimization, website analytics, social media guidance, and content generation capabilities for small and medium businesses.
Flable.summary_abuse: No abuse or security incidents identified. Flable is a very early-stage startup founded in 2023. As a German company it operates under GDPR. No data misuse or deceptive practices reported. Very limited public information available due to early stage.

FlagSmith.history: Flagsmith (formerly Bullet Train) was founded around 2018 in the UK as an open-source feature flagging and remote config service. It provides both a self-hosted option and a hosted SaaS at app.flagsmith.com. The project is active on GitHub with a BSD-3-Clause licensed core and some enterprise features in paid tiers.
FlagSmith.summary_abuse: Flagsmith is a feature flag management tool with no documented abuse history. As developer infrastructure for controlling feature rollouts, it has no consumer-facing attack surface and is not a known abuse vector.

Flamp.history: Flamp is a Russian local business review platform developed by 2GIS, covering businesses in Russian cities. Launched around 2011, it provides ratings, reviews, and business profiles. It is part of the 2GIS mapping and local business ecosystem. The service is primarily used in Russia.
Flamp.summary_abuse: Flamp is a local reviews platform operating primarily in Russia. Review platforms can be exploited for fake reviews and reputation manipulation, which is a documented pattern in the Russian market. The platform is operated by 2GIS which has incentive to maintain review quality, but limited Western visibility means its anti-abuse posture is harder to verify.

FlareLane.history: FlareLane is a South Korean cross-channel customer engagement and push notification platform founded around 2020. It provides web push, mobile push, SMS, and in-app messaging for marketers. The company is based in Korea and serves primarily the Korean and Asian markets.
FlareLane.summary_abuse: FlareLane is a push notification marketing platform. Push notification services have known abuse potential for spam campaigns, and the category is associated with unsolicited notification patterns. The platform is a small Korean startup with limited public anti-abuse documentation.

Flarum.history: Flarum is an open-source PHP forum software conceived as the successor to esoTalk and FluxBB. Development began around 2014, and the first stable release (v1.0) came in May 2021 after 11 years of development. It is built with Laravel and Mithril.js and emphasizes extensibility through a rich extension ecosystem.
Flarum.summary_abuse: Flarum is self-hosted forum software with no centralized abuse surface. Like any forum platform, Flarum-powered communities could host spam or harmful content, but responsibility lies with instance operators. No notable platform-level abuse incidents documented.

Flashchat.history: Flashchat is a Web3 social media app by Flashtrend LLC, launched around 2022. It enables wallet-to-wallet messaging, tokenized group chats based on NFT ownership, Web3 activity feeds, and NFT display profiles. Users can sign in with a crypto wallet without email or phone. The app offers Flashcoins earned by referrals and is available on iOS and Android. It targets the Web3/crypto community as an alternative to traditional social networks.
Flashchat.summary_abuse: Flashchat is a Web3 social app with limited public track record. The crypto-focused nature raises concerns about financial speculation features and potential exposure to NFT scams. No formal security certifications. The app collects wallet data and social interactions. Privacy practices are unclear. Limited regulatory oversight typical of early-stage Web3 apps.

Flask.history: Flask was created by Armin Ronacher as an April Fools Day joke in 2010, but was quickly adopted as a serious Python micro web framework. It was built as a thin wrapper around Werkzeug (WSGI toolkit) and Jinja2 (template engine).
